courses.title: ChatGPT as Your Operating System
courses.description: Automation triggers, persistent context architecture, Zapier/Make integrations, and the maintenance habits that make ChatGPT compound across weeks and months — not just single sessions.

lessons.title: Zapier & Make: Automated Triggers
lessons.description: Use Zapier and Make to trigger ChatGPT automatically from events in your existing tools. Build workflows that run without you touching them.

sections.content: Most people use ChatGPT reactively — open a tab, ask a question. Power users build trigger-based workflows: a new form submission drafts a response, a new Slack message summarizes overnight activity, a spreadsheet update generates a report. That's ChatGPT running for you, not with you.

Zapier connects 7,000+ apps with no code. Make handles more complex branching and data transformation. Both can trigger a ChatGPT prompt when an event fires, then send the result somewhere useful. Decision: use Zapier for speed, Make when you need conditional logic or multi-step data processing.

Anatomy of a trigger workflow: Trigger (event in an app) → Action (send data + prompt to ChatGPT) → Output (send ChatGPT's response somewhere). Example: New Typeform submission → ChatGPT drafts a personalized response → Draft saved to Google Docs for human review.

Three starter workflows that pay off immediately: Support inbox triage (new email → ChatGPT categorizes + drafts reply → Slack notification), Meeting prep (calendar event tomorrow → ChatGPT pulls context + drafts agenda → sent to your email), Weekly digest (Friday trigger → ChatGPT summarizes Slack + email activity → sent as a brief).

The approval gate rule: every trigger workflow that sends something external (email, Slack, publish) must route through human review first. Automate the drafting. Approve the sending. A weekly digest that auto-sends without review will eventually send something wrong.

Build trigger workflows in order of stakes: internal (low risk) first, external (email, posts) second. Prove the trigger is reliable, the prompt produces good output, and the output goes to the right place before expanding. One solid workflow beats five broken ones.
Zapier for speed, Make for complex logic. Trigger → ChatGPT prompt → output. Approval gate before anything external sends. Build internal workflows first, prove them, then expand.

exercises.prompt: Match each automation need to the right trigger workflow component.

pairs.left: A new support ticket arrives in your helpdesk
pairs.right: Trigger — the event that starts the workflow

pairs.left: ChatGPT drafts a categorized response using the ticket text
pairs.right: Action — ChatGPT processes the trigger data

pairs.left: The draft is saved to a Google Doc for human review
pairs.right: Output — where the result goes before approval

pairs.left: You review and send the response from the Google Doc
pairs.right: Approval gate — human review before anything external sends

hints: Think about timing: what fires first, what processes it, where does it go, who approves?

exercises.prompt: You want to build a Zapier workflow that automatically drafts a reply when a new lead fills out a contact form. Walk through: what the trigger is, what data you pass to ChatGPT, what the prompt looks like, and where the draft goes.
exercises.evaluation_criteria: Should identify: Trigger = new Typeform/form submission. Data passed to ChatGPT = lead's name, company, message, and any context about your services. Prompt should include the lead data and ask for a personalized first response in a specific tone. Output should go to a draft location (Gmail drafts, Google Doc, or Slack channel) for human review — not auto-send. Should explain that auto-sending first responses to leads is too high-stakes to fully automate.
hints: What information from the form does ChatGPT need to personalize the reply?
Where should the draft land so you can review it before sending?

exercises.prompt: Design a weekly digest workflow using Make or Zapier. Describe: the trigger, what data sources it pulls from, the ChatGPT prompt you would use, and what the final output looks like.
exercises.evaluation_criteria: Must identify a specific trigger (Friday at 5pm, or similar scheduled trigger). Must name at least two data sources (Slack, email, project tracker, CRM, etc.). ChatGPT prompt must specify what to summarize, the length, the audience, and the format. Output must go somewhere reviewable (email, Slack DM, Google Doc) with explicit mention of human review before distribution. Should note the difference between Make and Zapier for this use case.
hints: What information from your week would you most want summarized?
Who is the audience for the digest — just you, or your team?

lessons.title: Context Documents as Persistent Memory
lessons.description: Build a context document system that makes every ChatGPT session start fully briefed — without burning tokens on history reconstruction.

sections.content: ChatGPT doesn't remember your last session. Every conversation starts fresh. Power users solve this with context documents: short, structured files you paste at session start that load all the context ChatGPT needs in one shot.

Three document types, each handles a different layer: Role doc (who you are, your role, your audience — permanent, changes rarely), Project doc (current status, key decisions, blockers — updated weekly), Decision log (record of choices made and why — append-only, never delete).

Role doc template: "I am [role] at [company type]. My audience is [audience]. I write for [platform]. My voice: [3 rules]. I never: [2 anti-patterns]." Under 100 words. Paste this at the start of any writing or strategy session.

Project doc template: "Project: [name]. Status: [current state]. Last decision: [decision + rationale]. In progress: [active task]. Blockers: [what's stuck]." Under 150 words. Update at the end of each work session. Paste at the start of the next.

Decision log format: append-only. Each entry: "[Date]: Decided [X] because [Y]. Alternative considered: [Z]." Ask ChatGPT to reference the log when a new decision connects to a past one. This is how you stop re-litigating the same decisions.

Maintenance: update the project doc at the end of every session that changes status (5 minutes). Monthly: review the role doc for drift. Quarterly: archive old decision log entries that are no longer relevant. Stale context is worse than none — ChatGPT will apply old context confidently.

Three docs: role (permanent), project (weekly), decision log (append-only). Paste the relevant ones at session start. Update the project doc at session end. Stale context = wrong answers confidently delivered.

exercises.prompt: You start a ChatGPT session to work on a proposal for a client you've been working with for three weeks. ChatGPT gives you generic output that ignores the context from previous sessions. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: ChatGPT forgot — start a new account.
You didn't paste your project context doc at session start. ChatGPT has no memory of previous conversations.
Use a longer prompt in this session.
exercises.explanation: ChatGPT does not retain memory between sessions by default. Without a context doc, every session starts blank. The fix: paste your project doc (current status, key decisions, client context) at the start of any work session. 15 seconds of pasting gives ChatGPT three weeks of context.
hints: What does ChatGPT know about your previous conversations with it?

exercises.prompt: You want ChatGPT to know you're a senior marketing manager, your audience is B2B CFOs, you write LinkedIn posts in a direct non-salesy voice, and you never use buzzwords. Where does this information belong?
options: Re-type it at the start of every conversation.
In your Custom Instructions (persistent, loads automatically) AND in a role context doc for detailed sessions.
In a saved ChatGPT conversation you can scroll back to.
In the ChatGPT memory — it will learn over time.
exercises.explanation: Custom Instructions loads automatically in every session — ideal for persistent role context. A role doc is useful for detailed sessions where you need to paste more specifics than Custom Instructions can hold. The two work together: Custom Instructions for the essentials, role doc for the details.
hints: Which approach loads without you doing anything? Which handles more detail?

exercises.prompt: You are starting a new work session on a product launch that's been in flight for two weeks. You have a project context doc but haven't updated it since Tuesday (it's Friday). Walk through: what you update before pasting, what you paste, and what you ask ChatGPT first to verify it understood the context.
exercises.evaluation_criteria: Should update the project doc with: new status, any decisions made since Tuesday, and current blockers. Then paste the updated doc. Then verify context by asking ChatGPT to summarize what it knows about the project — not to start working yet. This verification step catches missing context before it causes bad output three messages in. Should distinguish between the project doc (current state) and the decision log (historical choices).
hints: What changed since Tuesday that ChatGPT needs to know?
How do you verify ChatGPT understood your context before starting real work?

lessons.title: Building a Prompt Library
lessons.description: Capture your best prompts as reusable templates. Build a personal library that compounds over time and eliminates starting from scratch on recurring tasks.

sections.content: Most people type new prompts from scratch every session. Power users have a prompt library: a set of tested, reusable templates for their recurring tasks. When a prompt produces great output, capture it. Next time, paste it and swap in the new specifics.

When to capture a prompt: it produced output you'd actually use, it required three or more rounds of iteration to get right, or it's a task you do more than twice a month. Those three conditions together mean the template will pay back many times over.

Template format: mark variables with [BRACKETS]. Example: "Write a follow-up email to [NAME] who [ACTION]. Tone: [TONE]. Goal: [GOAL]. Under [WORD COUNT] words. Never: [ANTI-PATTERN]." Variables are what changes. Everything else is tested logic you don't want to rebuild.

Organize by job type, not by tool: Writing (emails, posts, reports), Analysis (contracts, data, research), Decision support (pre-mortems, comparisons, critiques), Operations (status updates, meeting prep, handoffs). A small, organized library beats a large unstructured one.
Store your library in Notion, Google Docs, or a plain text file — wherever you can paste from in under 10 seconds. The best storage system is the one you actually use. Complexity kills the habit.
Monthly review: scan your library for templates you haven't used in 30 days. Archive or delete them. A library of 10 prompts you use weekly beats 100 you never open. The signal is usage frequency.
Capture prompts that worked, mark variables with brackets, organize by job type. Store where you can paste in under 10 seconds. Monthly review: archive unused templates.

exercises.prompt: Match each scenario to the correct prompt library category.

pairs.left: You spend 20 minutes crafting the perfect cold email prompt after 4 iterations.
pairs.right: Capture it — multiple iterations means high template value

pairs.left: You ask ChatGPT a one-off question about a topic you'll never revisit.
A prompt that produces a weekly status report you send every Friday.
pairs.right: Capture it — recurring task with consistent format

pairs.left: You tried a prompt, it failed, and you had to rewrite it entirely.
pairs.right: Capture the fixed version — not the failed one

hints: The capture decision comes down to: will I need this again, and was it hard to get right?

exercises.prompt: You just got a great ChatGPT response for a performance review for one of your direct reports. The prompt took three rounds to get right. Walk through converting it into a reusable template for all future performance reviews.
exercises.evaluation_criteria: Should identify what's specific to this review (employee name, specific achievements, areas for growth) and what's structural logic (tone, format, what sections to include, what to avoid). Variables should be bracketed: [EMPLOYEE NAME], [KEY ACHIEVEMENTS], [DEVELOPMENT AREAS], [RATING]. The structural logic (what to include, how to frame development areas constructively, length constraints) stays fixed. Should note that the template is tested — it produced good output once, so the structure is validated.
hints: What parts of the prompt are specific to this employee vs. what would apply to every performance review?

exercises.prompt: Convert this specific prompt into a reusable template by identifying the variables and keeping the tested logic fixed. Original prompt: 'Write a LinkedIn post about the lesson I learned from losing a $200K client to a competitor. The competitor won because of pricing, not quality. My voice: direct, no buzzwords, first-person. Lead with the loss, not the lesson. Under 200 words. No engagement-bait ending.'
exercises.evaluation_criteria: Should produce a template that replaces the specifics with brackets: [LOSS/SETBACK], [ROOT CAUSE], [DOLLAR AMOUNT or METRIC] (optional), while keeping the structural logic fixed: voice rules (direct, no buzzwords, first-person), structure rule (lead with the loss not the lesson), length constraint (under 200 words), anti-pattern rule (no engagement-bait ending). The template should be general enough to use for any lesson-from-setback post, but specific enough to preserve what made the original prompt work.
hints: What changes between posts (the specific story) vs. what stays the same (how you want it written)?

lessons.title: The Full Operating System
lessons.description: Custom Instructions + Memory + context docs + prompt library + Zapier triggers assembled as a complete system. The compound effect and the weekly maintenance habit that keeps it working.

sections.content: The full system has four layers, each handling a different scope: Custom Instructions (who you are, how you want responses — permanent), Memory (durable facts ChatGPT saves across sessions), Context docs (current project state — updated weekly), Prompt library (tested templates for recurring tasks).

A mature morning workflow: open ChatGPT — Custom Instructions and Memory already loaded. Paste your project context doc (15 seconds). Open your prompt library, pick the template for today's work. Swap in the variables. First message is already 80% of the way to a great response.

Layer in one at a time: Week 1 — Custom Instructions. Week 2 — project context doc. Week 3 — start capturing prompts to your library. Week 4 — build your first Zapier trigger. Each layer becomes a habit before you add the next. All four at once is how you abandon all four.

The compound effect: Month 3, ChatGPT applies voice rules you set up in month 1 without reminders. Month 6, your prompt library handles 80% of your recurring tasks. Month 12, your Zapier triggers run daily workflows while you sleep. The system works harder as it matures.

Weekly maintenance (10 minutes): update your project context doc, add any new prompt templates you captured this week, check Zapier for failed workflows. Monthly maintenance (20 minutes): review Custom Instructions for drift, archive unused prompt templates, update Memory entries that are stale. Discipline at 10 minutes a week beats rebuilding from scratch every month.

Four layers: Custom Instructions, Memory, context docs, prompt library. Add one per week. Weekly 10-minute maintenance keeps the system current. The compound effect hits at month 3 and accelerates from there.

exercises.prompt: Match each layer of the operating system to its update frequency.

pairs.right: Rarely — when your role or core preferences change
Every session — updated at the end with new status and decisions
As needed — add when a prompt works, archive when unused for 30 days
Monthly — check for failures, update prompts when output drifts

hints: Think about how often the underlying information changes for each layer.

exercises.prompt: You built all four layers in one weekend: Custom Instructions, project context doc, prompt library with 20 templates, and two Zapier triggers. Two weeks later you have stopped updating the project doc and haven't used the prompt library. What went wrong?
options: The system is too complex to maintain.
You built everything at once instead of layering it in. Start with Custom Instructions, form the habit, then add the next layer.
Two weeks is too short to evaluate — keep going.
exercises.explanation: Building all four layers at once creates maintenance overhead before the habits are formed. The fix is incremental adoption: Custom Instructions first (form the habit of trusting them), then the project doc (form the habit of updating it), then the prompt library (form the habit of capturing), then triggers. Each layer becomes reflexive before the next arrives.
hints: How do habits form? All at once or one at a time?

exercises.prompt: A colleague asks you to help them set up this operating system from scratch. They have nothing configured today. Write a 4-week adoption plan: what do they build each week, what habit must be solid before moving to the next layer, and what does success look like at the end of week 4?
exercises.evaluation_criteria: Week 1: Set up Custom Instructions. Habit: trust that every session starts with your context loaded — stop re-explaining yourself. Success: 5 conversations that started without pasting any context. Week 2: Write a project context doc for one active project. Habit: paste it at every work session start, update it at session end. Success: used on 3 sessions with one update after each. Week 3: Start the prompt library with 3-5 templates from prompts that worked this week. Habit: when a prompt works, capture it. Success: used one saved template from the library at least twice. Week 4: Build one Zapier trigger for a high-frequency task (email triage, form responses, weekly digest). Habit: review Zapier dashboard once a week for failures. Success: trigger fired at least 3 times without manual intervention.
hints: Each week adds one layer. What habit from the previous week must be automatic before adding the next?
Define success for each week in terms of concrete behavior, not just setup.
